feature_name: Dual operation modes: Root Complex (RC) and End Point (EP)
description: The controller can operate either as a PCIe Root Complex or as an End Point, enabling flexible system topologies. RC mode manages downstream ports and enumerates attached endpoints. EP mode allows the device to appear as a peripheral to a host root complex. Mode selection impacts configuration flows, address decoding, and interrupt signaling. Selecting the correct mode early in boot is essential to system bring-up.
dependencies: Build-time or boot-time mode selection; board topology
Remarks: Choose RC or EP before link training; impacts BARs and interrupt roles

feature_name: Single bidirectional link, up to x2 lane width
description: A single ingress and single egress port form one bidirectional PCIe link. The link supports up to two lanes operating in parallel for higher throughput. Lane aggregation follows standard PCIe link training and width negotiation. The hardware exposes one logical port only. System design must route up to two differential lane pairs to the PHY.
dependencies: SerDes lane availability; board routing; link training
Remarks: Configure lane width to match board wiring

feature_name: PCIe link speeds: 2.5 and 5.0 Gbps per lane
description: The controller operates at Gen1 (2.5 GT/s) and Gen2 (5.0 GT/s) raw rates per lane in each direction. Effective throughput depends on encoding overhead and payload sizes. Link speed is negotiated during training with the partner. System firmware may constrain allowed speeds for signal-integrity margins. Higher speeds require compliant PCB layout and clocking.
dependencies: Gen1/Gen2 capable PHY; signal integrity; partner capability
Remarks: Optionally limit speed for stability during bring-up

description: Outbound TLP payloads are limited to 128 bytes. This bound affects DMA and CPU initiated write throughput. Software should coalesce transactions to align with payload limits. Larger application buffers require segmentation across multiple TLPs. Exceeding this size is not supported by the controller in outbound direction.
dependencies: PCIe Max Payload Size settings; egress path
Remarks: Tune Max Payload Size to 128B in configuration space

description: Inbound TLP payloads up to 256 bytes are accepted. This larger inbound size improves efficiency for host-to-device transfers. Link partners may need configuration to match this limit. Software should consider buffering to efficiently handle 256-byte chunks. Violating this limit can cause flow-control backpressure or errors.
dependencies: PCIe Max Payload Size settings; ingress path; partner config
Remarks: Advertise and negotiate 256B inbound payload capability

feature_name: Maximum remote read request size: 256 bytes
description: The largest supported read request size is 256 bytes. This impacts the number of completions required for large device reads. Software-initiated reads should be sized accordingly for best efficiency. Mismatch with the partner's completion size affects performance. Configure the Max Read Request Size field to comply with this bound.
dependencies: Config space Max Read Request Size; partner completion size
Remarks: Set MRRS to 256B to avoid protocol violations

description: The controller is architected for minimal path latency through the PCIe stack. Reduced latency benefits real-time and high-throughput applications. Actual latency depends on link conditions, payload sizes, and buffering. Drivers should minimize small fragmented transactions to sustain low latency. Measurement should be validated on the target board.
dependencies: Link conditions; payload sizing; buffering strategy
Remarks: Performance attribute; no special enable required

description: The link can train and operate at narrower widths than the routed maximum. Automatic negotiation selects a common width with the partner. This enables graceful operation when one lane is impaired. Software can query negotiated width via capability registers. Recovery to wider width may require link retrain.
dependencies: Training state machine; partner lane availability
Remarks: Monitor negotiated width and retrain if necessary

description: Lane ordering can be automatically inverted to accommodate PCB routing. The controller detects reversed lane ordering during training. This eases board design constraints without software changes. No special driver sequencing is required to benefit. Validation should still confirm correct polarity and mapping.
Remarks: Feature is handled in hardware during link training

description: The receiver supports automatic polarity inversion handling. This allows differential pair polarity to be swapped on the PCB if needed. Hardware corrects polarity without software overhead. Enablement is typically via PHY configuration defaults. Signal integrity must still meet PCIe specifications.
Remarks: Confirm PHY defaults match board polarity requirements

description: Only one virtual channel is implemented. Traffic arbitration across VCs is not available. Software cannot create or configure additional VCs. QoS schemes relying on multi-VC must be implemented differently. Plan traffic management using other mechanisms such as TC and flow control.
Remarks: Design assumes a single VC (VC0) only

description: Only one traffic class is supported. Priority differentiation via multiple TCs is not available. All TLPs share the same TC. System-level QoS must be achieved using alternative methods. This simplifies configuration but limits prioritization granularity.
Remarks: Assume TC0 only and avoid TC-based QoS

description: When configured as an End Point, the device exposes exactly one PCI function. Multi-function endpoint operation is not supported. Enumeration and BAR allocation are simplified. Software should not attempt to configure additional functions. RC drivers can rely on a single-function enumeration path.
dependencies: EP mode selection; function number fields
Remarks: Plan BAR usage for a single EP function

description: Flow control credits are handled by hardware without driver micromanagement. This ensures correct TLP pacing under varying link conditions. Software should still avoid excessive outstanding requests. Proper configuration of payload and request sizes complements credit handling. Telemetry can be used to monitor backpressure events if exposed.
Remarks: Rely on hardware credits; avoid overwhelming the link

description: The controller can append and verify end-to-end CRC for TLPs. Enabling ECRC enhances data integrity across the fabric. Partners must also support or tolerate ECRC for interoperability. Error events should be logged and may trigger AER reporting. Performance impact is minimal but measurable.
Remarks: Enable ECRC if the system validates compatibility

feature_name: PCI device power management (except D3 cold with Vaux)
description: Standard PCI power states are supported except D3 cold retention using auxiliary power. Transition handling follows PCIe power management rules. Wake signaling depends on platform wiring and PME policy. Drivers should manage state transitions for power savings. Persistent state across D3 cold is not available.
dependencies: PM capability; PME policy; platform Vaux
Remarks: Do not rely on Vaux for D3 cold state retention

description: Active State Power Management supports L0s and L1 states. Entry and exit latencies depend on PHY and board design. ASPM must be coordinated with the root complex policy. Improper settings can increase latency or cause instability. Validate ASPM behavior under real workloads.
dependencies: RC ASPM policy; link partner support
Remarks: Gate ASPM enablement behind stability testing

description: Standard link power states are implemented with the exception of L2. Lower-power link states reduce idle power. Not supporting L2 limits deepest idle capabilities. Software should not attempt to enter L2. Use L1 for significant savings with faster recovery.
Remarks: Avoid L2 requests; target L1 for idle periods

description: The controller implements PCIe Advanced Error Reporting capabilities. AER provides standardized logging and signaling for correctable and uncorrectable errors. Software should enable AER and register appropriate handlers. Error severity drives recovery actions such as link retraining or device reset. Integrate AER logs with system diagnostics.
dependencies: AER capability structure; error handler hooks
Remarks: Enable AER and route interrupts to system logger

description: Message TLPs are supported in both directions. This enables standard services such as power management events and error signaling. Message routing follows PCIe specifications. Software must configure acceptance and generation policies as needed. Excessive messages should be rate-limited if possible.
Remarks: Use messages for PME/AER as per platform policy

description: Hardware can filter different TLP classes for policy enforcement. Posted writes, non-posted requests, and completions can be independently permitted or blocked. This supports isolation of unintended traffic patterns. Filters should be configured during initialization. Misconfiguration can cause functional hangs.
Remarks: Define and test filter policies per deployment

feature_name: Configurable BAR, I/O, and configuration filtering; completion lookup/timeout
description: BAR-based address decoding and filtering are programmable. I/O and configuration access filtering provide additional control over allowed transactions. Completion lookup and timeout handling protect against stalled flows. Proper timeout values balance responsiveness with false triggers. Filters must respect the limitation on inbound I/O accesses.
dependencies: BAR setup registers; filter controls; timeout registers
Remarks: Tune completion timeouts conservatively at first

feature_name: Config space and external app MMR access via BAR0 and config
description: Software can access internal configuration space and external application registers. BAR0 maps to memory-mapped registers for efficient control. Configuration cycles can also access needed registers when permitted. Proper enumeration assigns BAR0 size and location. Security and isolation depend on system-level address mapping.
Remarks: Size BAR0 appropriately to expose needed MMRs

feature_name: Legacy interrupts: RC reception and EP generation
description: Legacy INTx signaling is supported per PCIe conventions. In RC mode, the controller can receive legacy interrupt events from endpoints. In EP mode, it can generate legacy interrupts toward the host. Software must configure masking and status handling. Prefer MSI where available for scalability.
Remarks: Use INTx only when MSI/MSI-X is unavailable

description: Message Signaled Interrupts are supported in both directions. MSI reduces pin count and improves interrupt delivery efficiency. Configuration requires programming the MSI capability structure and data payload. Host and device must agree on MSI vector allocation. MSI is preferred over legacy INTx for modern systems.
evidence: SPRUGS6D §1.3 Features; SPRUGS6D Fig 3-147..3-150 (MSI_CAP, MSI_LOW32, MSI_UP32, MSI_DATA)
dependencies: MSI capability enable; vector allocation; RC/EP role
Remarks: Enable MSI after BARs and address windows are valid

description: A receiver-to-transmitter loopback is available for link diagnostics when in RC mode. Loopback assists validation and signal integrity testing. It should be used in controlled environments to avoid impacting traffic. Software must place the link into the appropriate test state. Exit loopback before resuming normal operation.
Remarks: Use during manufacturing and bring-up tests only

feature_name: Limitation: x2 link cannot be split into two x1 links
description: The controller cannot expose two independent x1 links from a x2 configuration. Only a single logical port is available. System designs requiring two ports need external switching. Software should not attempt multi-port configurations. Enumeration must treat the controller as a single port entity.
dependencies: Hardware topology fixed to one port
Remarks: Architect for a single-port device only

feature_name: Limitation: No support for multiple VCs
description: Multiple virtual channels are not implemented. Traffic separation via multi-VC is impossible. Any QoS must be achieved without relying on VC features. Attempting to configure extra VCs will fail. System-level design must accommodate this constraint.
Remarks: Use other QoS mechanisms if needed

feature_name: Limitation: No support for multiple TCs
description: Only one traffic class is present. Priority tagging using TC1+ is not available. All traffic uses the same scheduling class. Driver should not program multiple TCs. Platform QoS must rely on alternative methods.
Remarks: Avoid TC-based differentiation

description: Function-level reset capability is not supported. Recovery from severe errors may require full device reset. Software must avoid assuming FLR semantics. Error handling should plan for link retrain or device-wide reset. Coordination with the RC is required for recovery.
Remarks: Implement device-wide reset paths instead

feature_name: Limitation: No PCIe beacon for in-band wake
description: In-band beacon signaling for wake is unsupported. Systems requiring wake must use alternative methods. PME and sideband signals are typical alternatives. Software should not enable beacon features. Power management flows must be adjusted accordingly.
Remarks: Rely on PME or GPIO-based wake mechanisms

feature_name: Limitation: No built-in hot plug support
description: Hardware lacks native hot plug controller functions. Dynamic insertion/removal must be handled by external logic if required. Software cannot rely on standard hot plug events from this controller. System design should avoid surprise hot-plug scenarios. Use fixed topology or external switch with hot-plug support.
Remarks: Design for static connections or add external HPC

description: Vendor-defined message TLPs are not supported. Only standard PCIe messages are allowed. Software should not transmit or expect vendor messages. Interoperability depends on using standardized mechanisms. Custom protocols over vendor messages are not possible here.
dependencies: Message routing limited to standard types
Remarks: Use standard messages like PME/AER only

feature_name: Limitation: No inbound I/O space access (RC or EP)
description: Inbound transactions targeting I/O space are not supported. The controller will not accept inbound I/O cycles in either mode. Memory or configuration spaces must be used instead. Systems relying on legacy I/O space must adapt. Filtering configuration should reflect this restriction.
dependencies: I/O space access path absent inbound
Remarks: Map resources to memory space, not I/O

feature_name: Limitation: Only incremental addressing for bursts; non-cacheable
description: Burst transactions support only incremental addressing. Other addressing modes are not available. As a result, PCIe addresses cannot be in cacheable memory regions. Software must avoid cacheable mappings for these windows. DMA and CPU accesses should use non-cacheable attributes.
Remarks: Mark PCIe regions as device/strongly-ordered

feature_name: Limitation: No auxiliary power retention for D3 cold
description: The controller cannot retain state in D3 cold via Vaux. On power restoration, full reinitialization is required. Wake from D3 cold must be treated as a fresh boot. Persistent configuration should be stored elsewhere. Power sequencing must consider this behavior.
Remarks: Plan full reinit on D3 cold exit

description: The deepest L2 link state is not supported. The controller cannot enter L2 even if requested. Power management should rely on L1 for deep idle. Attempting L2 may fail or be ignored. Validate power behavior accordingly.
Remarks: Target L1 for deepest supported idle

description: The device implements the standard MSI capability registers for programming MSI. Software must write the 32/64-bit address and data fields before enabling MSI. Proper ordering ensures that interrupts are not lost. The controller can both generate and receive MSI depending on mode. Vector management must match system allocation.
dependencies: Correct programming order; RC/EP role; vector mask
Remarks: Enable MSI only after target address is valid

description: The PCIe capabilities register exposes link and device capabilities. Software discovers negotiated width, speed, and features through this structure. Correct parsing drives subsequent configuration. Capability bits must be read-only or writable as specified. Misinterpretation can lead to suboptimal settings.
Remarks: Read capability chain early during init

description: Hardware tracks outstanding requests and detects completion timeouts. On timeout, status is updated and errors may be signaled via AER or interrupts. Timeout values should be chosen to balance latency and false positives. Software should implement retry or recovery policies. Monitoring helps identify fabric issues.
Remarks: Start conservative; tighten after validation

description: Inbound transactions can be remapped to internal address spaces using four region-specific controls. Each region programs BAR match, start address high/low, and an internal offset to define translation. Address Space 0 handles configuration and local registers while Address Space 1 handles data transfer. BAR1 can be designated as reference for translation with constraints. Transactions exceeding a region boundary are not automatically continued into the next region.
Remarks: Size regions to avoid boundary crossings; prefer 64-bit start fields when needed

description: The PCIe configuration space is 1 KB with fixed offsets independent of access mode. Type 1 configuration is used in RC mode and Type 0 in EP mode. Capability structures are discovered via the standard capability list. Software must follow the PCIe rules for config transactions per mode. This uniform layout simplifies driver access to capability and control registers.
dependencies: Mode-specific Type0/Type1 rules; capability list parsing
Remarks: Abstract config access to handle both RC and EP variants

description: The recommended RC initialization sequence requires enabling power/clock domains, programming the SerDes PLL, and controlling the link training state machine. Link training is disabled via CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN]=0 during configuration and enabled when ready. Successful training is indicated by DEBUG0.LTSSM_STATE reaching 0x11. Enumeration and endpoint handshakes follow before DMA starts.
Remarks: Follow documented order; gate LTSSM until config complete

description: The SerDes requires a reference clock and PLL configuration before link operation. Software programs PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL and waits for PCIE_SERDES_STS[LOCK]=1. Proper PLL setup is critical for signal integrity at Gen1/Gen2 speeds. Misconfiguration prevents successful link training. Monitoring lock status ensures readiness before enabling LTSSM.
dependencies: Reference clock source; SerDes registers; lock status
Remarks: Verify reference clock stability before enabling PLL

description: During EP initialization, APP_RETRY_EN in CMD_STATUS can force retry responses to defer servicing incoming accesses. This gives software time to complete configuration without causing timeouts at the root. Once initialization is finished, the bit must be cleared to accept normal traffic. This helps integrate slow setup sequences safely. Overuse may stall bus transactions.
Remarks: Enable briefly during critical init windows only

feature_name: Outbound transactions require Bus Master Enable
description: For outbound requests from EP to be serviced, the endpoint must be granted bus master capability by the root complex. Without this, outbound TLPs will be blocked. Drivers must set the Bus Master Enable bit during enumeration. This requirement applies before initiating DMA or CPU-originated PCIe reads/writes. It ensures controlled access to the PCIe fabric.
dependencies: Command register Bus Master Enable; RC policy
Remarks: Confirm BME is set before starting outbound traffic

description: In EP mode, the PCIe controller can only act as a target for configuration accesses. It cannot initiate config cycles as an endpoint. This shapes how software performs capability discovery and register programming. RC-originated config transactions reach the EP for enumeration. Device-side code should rely on memory-mapped control for self-configuration.
Remarks: Design init flows assuming EP is config target only

description: The DEBUG0 register exposes the LTSSM state machine status, enabling firmware to verify link progression. A value of 0x11 indicates successful training to L0. Polling this field supports robust bring-up sequencing. It can also assist in diagnosing link failures or marginal SI. Clear documentation of state values aids automation.
Remarks: Log LTSSM transitions during validation and errors

feature_name: Power/clock domain enable for PCIe module
description: The PCIe block must have its power and clock domains enabled before configuration. Device-level control (e.g., DEVSTAT.PCIESSEN) is used to bring the module out of reset/power-gate. Clocks must be running prior to SerDes PLL configuration. Skipping this step prevents any register access. Defer link training until domains are stable.
dependencies: PSC/user guide; DEVSTAT bits; clock sources
Remarks: Sequence power/clock before any PCIe register access

feature_name: Address Space 0 vs Address Space 1 usage
description: Two internal bus address spaces segment control versus data traffic. Address Space 0 is dedicated for configuration and local application registers, while Address Space 1 is dedicated to data transfer. Inbound translation rules select the target space per region. This separation helps isolate control paths from bulk data movement. Software should map windows accordingly.
Remarks: Place MMRs in AS0 and data buffers in AS1

description: The cited specification sections do not describe dedicated security primitives or access control for PCIe transactions. No features such as ACS, SR-IOV isolation, or TLP encryption are referenced in the available content. Security therefore relies on platform-level mechanisms and standard PCIe semantics. Use an IOMMU/SMMU for DMA isolation where available. Treat security capabilities as UNKNOWN until full register map review confirms details.
Remarks: Apply system-level isolation; verify on full spec

description: The available sections do not indicate an integrated DMA engine within the PCIe block. Data movement is presumed to be initiated by external masters (CPU or SoC DMA). Buffering is managed via PCIe flow control credits. Performance depends on the external DMA and memory subsystem. Treat the presence of a dedicated DMA engine as UNKNOWN until verified.
dependencies: External DMA (e.g., SoC EDMA); memory bandwidth
Remarks: Plan to use platform DMA for bulk transfers
